comment_text.sn12.65:1.2: This process describes the Buddha’s reflection before his awakening. It is of particular interest because it seems to depict the precise moment when his insight broke away from that of his Upaniṣadic teachers. Bhikkhu Bodhi notes: “It it possible the Bodhisatta had been seeking a self of the Upaniṣadic type, a self-subsistent subject consisting of pure consciousness that requires nothing but itself in order to exist. His discovery that consciousness is invariably dependent on name-and-form would have disclosed to him the futility of such a quest and thereby shown that even consciousness, the subtlest basis for the sense of self (see SN 12.61), is conditioned and thus marked by impermanence, suffering, and selflessness.”
comment_text.sn12.65:2.12: This analysis agrees with Yājñavalkya, who says that the manifold appearances in the world arise from consciousness (etebhyo bhūtebhyaḥ samutthāya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.12).
comment_text.sn12.65:2.16: Here the Buddha contradicts Yājñavalkya’s view that individuated awareness (saññā) returns into “this great reality, infinite, unbounded, a sheer mass of consciousness” (idaṁ mahadbhūtam anantam apāraṁ vijñānaghana eva, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.12). Consciousness (viññāṇa) is not a fundamental reality (mahadbhūta) underlying multiplicity, but rather a conditioned process that exists only together with name and form.
comment_text.sn12.65:3.2: The same idea is found at DN 14:2.19.2, where it also describes the reflective process of a Bodhisatta. The mutual conditioning of consciousness with name and form is then further detailed at DN 15:21.1 and SN 12.67. It seems that this distinctive presentation was the original form for describing a Bodhisatta’s thought process. It was subsequently applied to the full set of twelve links (SN 12.4).
comment_text.sn12.65:6.2: This phrase is found only here and DN 14:2.21.2.
comment_text.sn12.65:7.1: Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.4.8 quotes an ancient verse to the effect: “I have reached the subtle, extensive, ancient road; it has been realized by me”. | Añjasa is elsewhere in Pali used only in poetry (eg, Thag 1.35:1.3) or by brahmins (DN 13:4.2). It is an old Vedic term for the “direct route” to the right place as advised by Pūṣan (Rig Veda 6.54.1). In the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, the new and full moon offerings are made “directly and expressly” to Indra and Agni (2.4.17); one goes “directly to the truth” after a dispute over supremacy among the gods is resolved (3.4.2.14); the horse knows the “direct” way to heaven that humans do not, like one who knows the country (13.2.3–2); and Yājñavalkya explains how to go “directly” to complete the sacrifice (13.5.3.5). One beholds “directly” that the self is god (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.4.15).
comment_text.sn12.65:7.3: The idea of such “lost cities” decaying in the jungle is probably sparked by the archaeological remains of the Indus Valley Civilization, whose extensive cities would have been over a thousand years old in the Buddha’s day. One major site, Rakhigarhi, is a few day’s walk from Kammāsadamma, where the Buddha taught several times. He could easily have seen it himself or else heard about it.

root_text.sn12.65:1.2: “Pubbe me, bhikkhave, sambodhā anabhisambuddhassa bodhisattasseva sato etadahosi:
root_text.sn12.65:1.3: ‘kicchā vatāyaṁ loko āpanno jāyati ca jīyati ca mīyati ca cavati ca upapajjati ca.
root_text.sn12.65:1.4: Atha ca panimassa dukkhassa nissaraṇaṁ nappajānāti jarāmaraṇassa.
root_text.sn12.65:1.5: Kudāssu nāma imassa dukkhassa nissaraṇaṁ paññāyissati jarāmaraṇassā’ti?
root_text.sn12.65:1.7: ‘kimhi nu kho sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, kiṁpaccayā jarāmaraṇan’ti?
root_text.sn12.65:1.8: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:1.9: ‘jātiyā kho sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, jātipaccayā jarāmaraṇan’ti.
root_text.sn12.65:2.11: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:2.12: ‘viññāṇe kho sati nāmarūpaṁ hoti, viññāṇapaccayā nāmarūpan’ti.
root_text.sn12.65:2.14: ‘kimhi nu kho sati viññāṇaṁ hoti, kiṁpaccayā viññāṇan’ti?
root_text.sn12.65:2.15: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:2.16: ‘nāmarūpe kho sati viññāṇaṁ hoti, nāmarūpapaccayā viññāṇan’ti.
root_text.sn12.65:3.2: paccudāvattati kho idaṁ viññāṇaṁ nāmarūpamhā na paraṁ gacchati.
root_text.sn12.65:3.3: Ettāvatā jāyetha vā jīyetha vā mīyetha vā cavetha vā upapajjetha vā, yadidaṁ nāmarūpapaccayā viññāṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.65:3.8: ‘Samudayo, samudayo’ti kho me, bhikkhave, pubbe ananussutesu dhammesu cakkhuṁ udapādi ñāṇaṁ udapādi paññā udapādi vijjā udapādi āloko udapādi.
root_text.sn12.65:4.2: ‘kimhi nu kho asati, jarāmaraṇaṁ na hoti;
root_text.sn12.65:4.4: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:4.8: ‘kimhi nu kho asati jāti na hoti …pe…
root_text.sn12.65:4.17: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:5.2: ‘kimhi nu kho asati viññāṇaṁ na hoti;
root_text.sn12.65:5.4: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:
root_text.sn12.65:6.2: adhigato kho myāyaṁ maggo bodhāya yadidaṁ—
root_text.sn12.65:6.8: ‘Nirodho, nirodho’ti kho me, bhikkhave, pubbe ananussutesu dhammesu cakkhuṁ udapādi ñāṇaṁ udapādi paññā udapādi vijjā udapādi āloko udapādi.
root_text.sn12.65:7.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso araññe pavane caramāno passeyya purāṇaṁ maggaṁ purāṇañjasaṁ pubbakehi manussehi anuyātaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:7.3: Tamanugacchanto passeyya purāṇaṁ nagaraṁ purāṇaṁ rājadhāniṁ pubbakehi manussehi ajjhāvuṭṭhaṁ ārāmasampannaṁ vanasampannaṁ pokkharaṇīsampannaṁ uddhāpavantaṁ ramaṇīyaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:7.4: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, puriso rañño vā rājamahāmattassa vā āroceyya:
root_text.sn12.65:7.5: ‘yagghe, bhante, jāneyyāsi—ahaṁ addasaṁ araññe pavane caramāno purāṇaṁ maggaṁ purāṇañjasaṁ pubbakehi manussehi anuyātaṁ tamanugacchiṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:7.6: Tamanugacchanto addasaṁ purāṇaṁ nagaraṁ purāṇaṁ rājadhāniṁ pubbakehi manussehi ajjhāvuṭṭhaṁ ārāmasampannaṁ vanasampannaṁ pokkharaṇīsampannaṁ uddhāpavantaṁ ramaṇīyaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:7.8: Atha kho so, bhikkhave, rājā vā rājamahāmatto vā taṁ nagaraṁ māpeyya.
root_text.sn12.65:7.9: Tadassa nagaraṁ aparena samayena iddhañceva phītañca bāhujaññaṁ ākiṇṇamanussaṁ vuddhivepullappattaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:7.10: Evameva khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, addasaṁ purāṇaṁ maggaṁ purāṇañjasaṁ pubbakehi sammāsambuddhehi anuyātaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:8.1: Katamo ca so, bhikkhave, purāṇamaggo purāṇañjaso pubbakehi sammāsambuddhehi anuyāto?
root_text.sn12.65:8.4: Ayaṁ kho so, bhikkhave, purāṇamaggo purāṇañjaso pubbakehi sammāsambuddhehi anuyāto, tamanugacchiṁ;
root_text.sn12.65:8.24: Tadabhiññāya ācikkhiṁ bhikkhūnaṁ bhikkhunīnaṁ upāsakānaṁ upāsikānaṁ.
root_text.sn12.65:8.25: Tayidaṁ, bhikkhave, brahmacariyaṁ iddhañceva phītañca vitthārikaṁ bāhujaññaṁ puthubhūtaṁ yāva devamanussehi suppakāsitan”ti.

translation_text.sn12.65:1.2: “Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought:
translation_text.sn12.65:1.3: ‘Alas, this world has fallen into trouble. It’s born, grows old, dies, passes away, and is reborn,
translation_text.sn12.65:1.4: yet it doesn’t understand how to escape from this suffering, from old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.65:1.5: Oh, when will an escape be found from this suffering, from old age and death?’
translation_text.sn12.65:1.7: ‘When what exists is there old age and death? What is a requirement for old age and death?’
translation_text.sn12.65:1.8: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:1.9: ‘When rebirth exists there’s old age and death. Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.’
translation_text.sn12.65:2.10: What is a requirement for name and form?’
translation_text.sn12.65:2.11: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:2.12: ‘When consciousness exists there are name and form. Consciousness is a requirement for name and form.’
translation_text.sn12.65:2.14: ‘When what exists is there consciousness? What is a requirement for consciousness?’
translation_text.sn12.65:2.15: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:2.16: ‘When name and form exist there’s consciousness. Name and form are requirements for consciousness.’
translation_text.sn12.65:3.2: This consciousness turns back from name and form, and doesn’t go beyond that.
translation_text.sn12.65:3.3: This is the extent to which one may be reborn, grow old, die, pass away, or reappear. That is: name and form are requirements for consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.65:3.4: Consciousness is a requirement for name and form.
translation_text.sn12.65:3.5: Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields.
translation_text.sn12.65:3.6: The six sense fields are requirements for contact. …
translation_text.sn12.65:3.7: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.65:3.8: ‘Origination, origination.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another.
translation_text.sn12.65:4.2: ‘When what doesn’t exist is there no old age and death?
translation_text.sn12.65:4.3: When what ceases do old age and death cease?’
translation_text.sn12.65:4.4: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:4.5: ‘When rebirth doesn’t exist there is no old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.65:4.6: When rebirth ceases old age and death cease.’
translation_text.sn12.65:4.8: ‘When what doesn’t exist is there no rebirth …
translation_text.sn12.65:4.16: When what ceases do name and form cease?’
translation_text.sn12.65:4.17: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:4.18: ‘When consciousness doesn’t exist there is no name and form.
translation_text.sn12.65:4.19: When consciousness ceases name and form cease.’
translation_text.sn12.65:5.2: ‘When what doesn’t exist is there no consciousness?
translation_text.sn12.65:5.3: When what ceases does consciousness cease?’
translation_text.sn12.65:5.4: Then, through rational application of mind, I penetrated with wisdom:
translation_text.sn12.65:5.5: ‘When name and form don’t exist, there is no consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.65:5.6: When name and form cease, consciousness ceases.’
translation_text.sn12.65:6.2: I have discovered the path to awakening. That is:
translation_text.sn12.65:6.3: When name and form cease, consciousness ceases.
translation_text.sn12.65:6.4: When consciousness ceases, name and form cease.
translation_text.sn12.65:6.5: When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease.
translation_text.sn12.65:6.6: When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. …
translation_text.sn12.65:6.7: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.
translation_text.sn12.65:6.8: ‘Cessation, cessation.’ Such was the vision, knowledge, wisdom, realization, and light that arose in me regarding teachings not learned before from another.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.1: Suppose a person was walking through a forest. They’d see an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by humans in the past.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.3: they’d see an ancient city, an ancient capital, inhabited by humans in the past. It was lovely, complete with parks, groves, lotus ponds, and embankments.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.4: Then that person would inform a king or their chief minister:
translation_text.sn12.65:7.5: ‘Please sir, you should know this. While walking through a forest I saw an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by humans in the past.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.6: Following it along I saw an ancient city, an ancient capital, inhabited by humans in the past. It was lovely, complete with parks, groves, lotus ponds, and embankments.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.7: Sir, you should rebuild that city!’
translation_text.sn12.65:7.8: Then that king or their chief minister would have that city rebuilt.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.9: And after some time that city was successful and prosperous, populous, full of people, attained to growth and expansion.
translation_text.sn12.65:7.10: In the same way, I saw an ancient path, an ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.1: And what is that ancient path, the ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past?
translation_text.sn12.65:8.2: It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn12.65:8.3: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.4: This is that ancient path, the ancient route traveled by fully awakened Buddhas in the past.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.5: Following it along, I directly knew old age and death,
translation_text.sn12.65:8.8: and the practice that leads to their cessation.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.10: Following it along, I directly knew rebirth …
translation_text.sn12.65:8.20: Following it along, I directly knew choices,
translation_text.sn12.65:8.23: and the practice that leads to their cessation.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.24: Having directly known this, I told the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen.
translation_text.sn12.65:8.25: And that’s how this spiritual life has become successful and prosperous, extensive, popular, widespread, and well proclaimed wherever there are gods and humans.”

variant_text.sn12.65:7.3: ajjhāvuṭṭhaṁ → ajjhāvutthaṁ (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed) | uddhāpavantaṁ → uddāpaṁ (bj); uddāpavantaṁ (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
